user_query: Could you set up a small, self-contained scheduled runner like we used to hack together at MPII? Please implement a Python scheduler script at scripts/scheduled_runner.py that reads the schedule from input/schedule.yaml and repeatedly executes the command template by substituting {i} with 1..iterations, sleeping interval_seconds between runs. Use subprocess to run each command, time each run externally, and capture both stdout and stderr. Parse outputs as follows: (a) from stdout, extract METRIC accuracy and loss if present; (b) determine status=ok vs error from the exit code; and (c) if the run failed, parse the Python exception type from the traceback (e.g., ZeroDivisionError, KeyError, ValueError) and capture the message fragment. For each distinct error type encountered, perform a live Internet search (use any public search engine) with a query of the form: Python <error_type> plus the most informative token from the message if present (e.g., KeyError missing). Save up to the first 5 organic result titles and URLs and a domain frequency breakdown into out/error_lookups/<error_slug>.json, where <error_slug> is the lowercase error type with non-alphanumerics replaced by underscores. Produce the following artifacts under the workspace: 1) out/logs/run_events.jsonl — one JSON object per run with fields: run_id, start_ts, end_ts, duration_ms, exit_code, status, parsed_metrics (accuracy, loss if any), error_type (if any), and a brief stderr_tail (last line of stderr). 2) out/reports/summary.json — aggregate stats across all runs with fields: total_runs, success_count, failure_count, success_rate (0–1), duration_ms {min, max, mean}, metrics_on_success {accuracy_mean, loss_mean}, and failures_by_error_type (mapping error_type to count). 3) out/error_lookups/<error_slug>.json — one file per error type encountered with fields: timestamp, query, engine_name (string you choose), results (list of up to 5 objects with title and url), and domain_counts (mapping domain to count). Please do not hardcode any URLs; the search step must be performed live. The run should stop after completing the configured number of iterations. Use only the provided inputs below for the scheduled command and configuration, and ensure your code runs in a normal local workspace without needing credentials or special services. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
